verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where was a synagogue of the Jews: and Paul, as his manner was, went in unto them, and three sabbath days reasoned with them out of the Scriptures, opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead; and that this Jesus, whom I preach unto you, is Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again they haste past the small cities, and press on to the greater ones, since from those the word was to flow as from a fountain into the neighboring cities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul, as his manner was, went into the synagogue of the Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Although he had said, “We turn to the Gentiles” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 46), he did not leave these alone: such was the longing affection he had towards them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For hear him saying, “Brethren, my heart’s desire and prayer to God for Israel is, that they might be saved” (Rom. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1): and, “I wished myself accursed from Christ for my brethren.” (ib. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he did this because of God’s promise and the glory: and this, that it might not be a cause of offence to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Opening,” it says, “from the Scriptures, he reasoned with them for three sabbaths, putting before them that the Christ must suffer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do thou mark how before all other things he preaches the Passion: so little were they ashamed of it, knowing it to be the cause of salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And some of them believed, and consorted with Paul and Silas; and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the chief women not a few.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The writer mentions only the sum and substance of the discoursing: he is not given to redundancy, and does not on every occasion report the sermons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But the Jews which believed not (the best texts omit “which believed not”), moved with envy, took unto them certain lewd fellows of the baser sort, and gathered a company, and set all the city on an uproar, and assaulted the house of Jason, and sought to bring them out to the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they found them not, they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city, crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also; whom Jason hath received: and these all do contrary to the decrees of Cæsar, saying that there is another king, one Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Oh! what an accusation! again they get up a charge of treason against them, “saying, there is another king (one) Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they troubled the people and the rulers of the city, when they heard these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had taken security of Jason, and of the other, they let them go.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A man worthy to be admired, that he put himself into danger, and sent them away from it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the brethren immediately sent away Paul and Silas by night unto Berea: who coming thither went into the synagogue of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These were more noble,” it says, “than they of Thessalonica: more noble,” i.e. more gentle (ἐπιεικέστεροι) (in their behavior): “in that they received the word with all readiness,” and this not inconsiderately, but with a strictness wherein was no passion, “searching the Scriptures whether these things were so.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore many of them believed; also of honorable women which were Greeks, and of men, not a few.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was preached of Paul at Berea, they came thither also, and stirred up the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then immediately the brethren sent away Paul to go as it were to the sea: but Silas and Timotheus abode there still.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he at one time gives way, at another presses on, and in many things takes his measures upon human considerations.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they that conducted Paul brought him unto Athens: and receiving a commandment unto Silas and Timotheus for to come to him with speed, they departed.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look again at what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Three sabbath-days,” it says, being the time when they had leisure from work, “he reasoned with them, opening out of the Scriptures” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2): for so used Christ also to do: as on many occasions we find Him reasoning from the Scriptures, and not on all occasions (urging men) by miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because to this indeed they stood in a posture of hostility, calling them deceivers and jugglers; but he that persuades men by reasons from the Scriptures, is not liable to this imputation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And on many occasions we find (Paul) to have convinced men simply by force of teaching: and in Antioch “the whole city was gathered together” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 44): so great a thing is this also, for indeed this itself is no small miracle, nay, it is even a very great one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that they might not think that they did it all by their own strength, but rather that God permitted it, two things resulted, namely, “Some of them were persuaded,” etc. (c) “And of devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the chief women not a few:” but those others did the contrary: “the Jews moved with envy,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4, 5) (b) and, from the fact that the being called was itself a matter of God’s fore-ordering, (a) they neither thought great things of themselves as if the triumph were their own, nor were terrified as being responsible (for all).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how comes it that he said, “That we should go unto the heathen, and they unto the circumcision” (Gal. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9), and yet discoursed to the Jews? (α) He did this as a thing over and above. (β) For he did other things also more than he was obliged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, Christ ordained that they should “live by the Gospel” (1 Cor. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), but our Apostle did it not: Christ sent him not to baptize, yet he did baptize.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how he was equal to all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Peter to the circumcision, he to the Gentiles, to the greater part. (α) Since if it was necessary for him to discourse to Jews, how said he again: “For He that wrought effectually in him toward the circumcision, the same was mighty also in me toward the Gentiles” (Gal. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same way as those Apostles also had intercourse with the Gentiles, though they had been set apart for the circumcision, so likewise did our Apostle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The more part of his work indeed was with the Gentiles: still he did not neglect the Jews either, that they might not seem to be severed from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how was it, you will ask, that he entered in the first place into the synagogues, as if this were his leading object?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True; but he persuaded the Gentiles through the Jews, and from the things which he discoursed of to the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he knew, that this was most suitable for the Gentiles, and most conducive to belief.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore he says: “Inasmuch as I am the “Apostle of the Gentiles.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And his Epistles too all fight against the Jews.—“That the Christ,” he says, “must needs have suffered.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If there was a necessity for His suffering, there was assuredly a necessity for His rising again: for the former was far more wonderful than the latter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if He gave Him up to death Who had done no wrong, much rather did He raise Him up again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But the Jews which believed not took unto them certain of the baser sort, and set all the city on an uproar (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5): so that the Gentiles were more in number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews thought not themselves enough to raise the disturbance: for because they had no reasonable pretext, they ever effect such purposes by means of uproar, and by taking to themselves base men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they found them not,” it says, “they haled Jason and certain brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O the tyranny! dragged them without any cause out of their houses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “These all,” say they, “do contrary to the decrees of Cæsar” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7): for since they spoke nothing contrary to what had been decreed, nor made any commotion in the city, they bring them under a different charge: “saying that there is another king, one Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they troubled the people,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what are ye afraid of, seeing He is dead? (β) “And when they had taken security,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how by giving security Jason sent Paul away: so that he gave his life (to the hazard) for him. (α) “And brethren,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how the persecutions in every case extend the preaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now these,” it says, “were more noble than those in Thessalonica” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11): i.e. they were not (men) practising base things, but some were convinced, and the others (who were not), did nothing (of that sort). (β) “Daily,” it says, “searching the Scriptures whether these things were so:” not merely upon a sudden impetus or (burst of) zeal.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “More noble,” it says: i. e. in point of virtue(α)“Therefore many of them,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here again are Greeks. (β) “But when the Jews of Thessalonica,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13), because there were lewd persons there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet that city was greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is no wonder in the greater city the people were worse: nay, of course to the greater city there go the worse men, where the occasions of disturbances are many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as in the body, where the disease is more violent for having more matter and fuel, just so is it here. (α) But look, I beg you, how their fleeing was providentially ordered, not from cowardice: otherwise they would have ceased to preach, and would not have exasperated them still more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But from this (flight) two things resulted: both the rage of those (Jews) was quenched, and the preaching spread.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in terms befitting their disorderly conduct, he says, “Agitating the multitude.” (β) Just what was done at Iconium—that they may have the additional condemnation of destroying others besides themselves. (ch. xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is what Paul says of them: “Forbidding to preach to the Gentiles, to fill up their sins alway, for the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Thess. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did he not stay? for if (at Lystra, ch. xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19, 21) there, where he was stoned, he nevertheless stayed a long time, much more here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (The Lord) did not wish them to be always doing signs; for this is itself a sign, not less than the working of signs—that being persecuted, they overcame without signs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that just as now He prevails without signs, so was it on many occasions His will to prevail then.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consequently neither did the Apostles run after signs: as in fact he says himself, “We preach Christ crucified” (1 Cor. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23)—to them that crave signs, to them that crave wisdom, we give that which cannot even after signs persuade, and yet we do persuade!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that this was a mighty sign.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See then, how when the preaching is extended, they are not in a hurry to run after signs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was right that thenceforth the believers should be mighty signs to the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit, by retreating and advancing they did these things. (α) “And immediately,” it says, “the brethren sent away Paul.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here now they send Paul alone: for it was for him they feared, lest he should suffer some harm, the head and front of all being in fact none other than he. (β) “They sent him away,” it says, “as it were to the sea:” that it might not be easy for them to seize him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For at present they could not have done much by themselves; and with him they accomplished and achieved many things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the present, it says, they wished to rescue him. (α) So far is it from being the case, that (supernatural) Grace worked all alike on all occasions: on the contrary, it left them to take their measures upon human judgment, (only) stirring them up and rousing them out of sleep, and making them to take pains.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, observe, it brought them safe only as far as Philippi, but no more after that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And receiving,” it says, “a commandment unto Silas and Timotheus for to come to him with all speed, they departed.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though he was a Paul, nevertheless he needed them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with good reason are they urged by God to go into Macedonia, for there lay Greece moreover bright (before them). (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what zeal the rest of the disciples showed with respect to their leaders: not as it is now with us, who are separated and divided into great and small: some of us exalted, while others are envious: for this is the reason why those are envious, because we are puffed up, because we will not endure to be put upon a par with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The reason why there is harmony in the body, is because there is no puffing up: and there is no puffing up, because the members are of necessity made to stand in need of each other, and the head has need of the feet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And God has made this to be the case with us, and, for all that, we will not endure it: although even without this, there ought to be love among us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear ye not how they that are without accuse us when they say, “Needs make friendships?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The laity have need of us; and we again exist for them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since teacher or ruler would not exist, if there were not persons to be taught, nor would he perform his part, for it would not be possible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As the land has need of the husbandman, and the husbandman of the land, so is it here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What reward is there for the teacher to receive, when he has none to produce that he has taught? and what for the taught, who have not had the benefit of the best teaching?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that we need each other alike in turn, both the governed, them that govern, and leaders, them that obey: for rulers are for the sake of many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since no one is sufficient to do anything by himself alone, whether need be to ordain (χειροτονἥσαι), or to examine men’s counsels and opinions, but they become more honorable by assembly and numbers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, the poor need givers, the givers again need receivers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Considering one another” he says, “to provoke unto love and to good works.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On this account the assembly of the whole Church has more power: and what each cannot do by himself singly, he is able to do when joined with the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore most necessary are the prayers offered up, here, for the world, for the Church, from the one end of the earth to the other, for peace, for those who are in adversities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul shows this when he says, “That for the gift bestowed upon us by the means of many persons thanks may be given by many on our behalf” (2 Cor. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11); that is, that He might confer the favor on many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And often he asks for their prayers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See also what God says with regard to the Ninevites: “And shall not I spare that city, wherein dwell more than six score thousand persons?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, “where two or three,” He says, “are gathered together in My Name” (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20), they prevail much, how much more, being many?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet thou mayest prevail, though thou be but one; yet not equally so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For why art thou but one?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou not make many?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou not become the maker of love?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou not create (κατασκευάζεις) friendship?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou lackest the chief excellence of virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as men’s being bad by agreement together more provokes God; so for men to be good by unanimity delights Him more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou shalt not follow a multitude,” He says, “to do evil.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They are all gone out of the way, they are together become unprofitable” (Rom. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12), and have become as it were men singing in concert in their wickedness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make for thyself friends in preference to domestics, and all besides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the peacemaker is a son of God, how much more he who makes friends also?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he who reconciles only is called a son of God, of what shall not he be worthy, who makes friends of those who are reconciled?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us engage ourselves in this trade, let us make those who are enemies to each other friends, and those who are not indeed enemies, but are not friends, them let us bring together, and before all, our own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as he who is at enmity in his house, and has differences with his wife, carries no authority when reconciling others, but will be told, “Physician, heal thyself” (Luke iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23), so will a man be told in this case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then is the enmity that is in us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That of the soul against the body, that of vice against virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This enmity let us put an end to, this war let us take away, and then being in peace we shall also address others with much boldness of speech, our conscience not accusing us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Anger fights against gentleness, love of money against contempt of it, envy against goodness of heart.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us make an end of this war, let us overthrow these enemies, let us set up these trophies, let us establish peace in our own city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have within us a city and a civil polity, and citizens and aliens many: but let us banish the aliens, that our own people may not be ruined.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let no foreign nor spurious doctrine enter in, no carnal desire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See we not that, if any enemy has been caught in a city, he is judged as a spy?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not only banish aliens, but let us drive out enemies also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we see one, let us deliver up to the ruler, (that is), to conscience (τᾥ νᾥ), that imagination which is indeed an alien, a barbarian, albeit tricked out with the garb of a citizen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are within us many imaginations of this kind, which are by nature indeed enemies, but are clad in sheep’s skins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as the Persians, when they have put off the tiara, and the drawers, and the barbarian shoes, and put on the other dress which is usual with us, and have shorn themselves close, and converse in our own tongue, conceal war under their outward garb: but once apply the tortures (βασάνους or “tests”), and thou bringest to light what is hidden: so here, examine (or “put to the test,”) by torture again and again such an imagination as this, and thou wilt quickly see that its spirit is that of a stranger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But to show you also by way of example the sort of spies which the devil sends into us to spy out what is in us, come let us strip one of them, and examine it strictly at the tribunal: and if you please, let us bring forward some of those which were detected by Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which things,” he says, “have indeed a show of wisdom in will-worship, and humility, and neglecting of the body: not in any honor to the satisfying of the flesh.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The devil wished to bring in Judaism: now if he had introduced it in its own form, he would not have carried his point.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly, mark how he brought it about.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “You must neglect the body,” he says: “this is (the true) philosophy, not to admit of meats, but to guard against them: this is humility.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now again in our own times, in the case of the heretics, he wished to bring us down to the creature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See then how he dressed up his deceit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had he said, “Worship a creature,” he would have been detected: but what says he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “God” (viz. the Son and the Holy Ghost), he says, “is a created being.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us lay bare for the decision of the judges the meaning of the Apostolic writings: there let us bring him: themselves will acknowledge both the preaching and the language.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many make gains “that they may have wherewith to give to the poor,” unjust gains: this too is a wicked imagination.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us undress it, let us convict it, that we may not be taken by it, but that having escaped all the devices of the devil, and holding to the sound doctrines with strictness, we may be able both to pass in safety through this life present, and to obtain the good things promised, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Ghost, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
